---
title: "Diffusion Policy for Compliant Robots"
subtitle: "From Imitation Learning to Variable Admittance Control"
author: "Alessandro Leanza"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    chalkboard: true
    incremental: false
    transition: slide
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    smaller: true
bibliography: references.bib
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Diffusion Policy for Compliant Robots

## Learning objectives

By the end of this lecture, you should understand:

- why imitation learning is useful for robotics;
- why simple behavioral cloning is often insufficient;
- how diffusion models generate samples through denoising;
- how Diffusion Policy generates action trajectories;
- why inference speed matters in robotic control;
- why compliant robots need impedance/admittance control;
- how Diffusion Policy can be combined with variable admittance control.

---

# Part I — Motivation

## Why learning from demonstrations?

Robots often need to solve tasks where designing a reward function is difficult, expensive, or unsafe.

Instead of specifying the task manually, we can collect expert demonstrations:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \{(o_t, a_t)\}_{t=1}^{N}
$$

where $o_t$ is the observation and $a_t$ is the expert action.

---

## The central question

Can we learn a policy:

$$
\pi_\theta(a_t | o_t)
$$

that reproduces expert behavior and generalizes to new situations?

---

# Part II — Diffusion Policy

## Key idea

Diffusion Policy does not generate a single action.

It generates a sequence of future actions:

$$
\mathbf{a}_{t:t+H}
\sim
p_\theta(\mathbf{a}_{t:t+H} | \mathbf{o}_{t-T_o:t})
$$

---

# Part III — Compliant Control

## Why compliance matters

In contact-rich robotics, the robot should not only decide where to move.

It should also decide how stiff or compliant it should be during interaction.

---

## Variable admittance control

A simple admittance model maps external forces into motion corrections:

$$
M_d \ddot{x}_r + D_d \dot{x}_r + K_d x_r = F_{ext}
$$
